overall_summary: An independent researcher proposes Project Phoenix, an open-source technical architecture for retrofitting data centers with closed-loop cooling, regenerative water systems, and grid-stabilizing solid-state transformers. The filing urges federal and state regulators to mandate open-source utility interfaces for data centers over 20 MW, requiring public ownership of critical infrastructure controls and prohibiting proprietary software-locking of core utility systems.

quotes.text: Corporate technology entities are monopolizing domestic power infrastructure, straining local aquifer levels, and increasing localized emissions via legacy diesel backup systems.
quotes.concern: Data centers monopolizing power, depleting aquifers, and emitting pollution

quotes.text: When left unregulated, these practices force local ratepayers to subsidize infrastructure upgrades while shouldering the long-term health, financial, and environmental liabilities.
quotes.concern: Ratepayers bear costs and liabilities that operators should pay

quotes.text: By replacing evaporative water consumption with closed supercritical CO2 cooling loops, implementing phase-change cryogenic freeze-crystallization for agricultural water purification, utilizing medium-voltage solid-state transformers for peak-shaving grid buffering, and implementing dynamic phase-inversion networks for biological EMF cancellation, this framework establishes a baseline for non-poisonous, high-density infrastructure computing.
quotes.concern: Technical solution suite for eliminating water consumption and emissions from data centers

quotes.text: Any federal, state, or municipal agency granting an interconnection or land-use permit for a data infrastructure asset exceeding 20 Megawatts must condition that approval upon the deployment of an Open-Source Utility Interface.
quotes.concern: Regulatory requirement for open-source controls on large data center permits

quotes.text: The public utility and local regulatory authority shall retain exclusive, sovereign ownership over the physical Solid-State Transformer (SST) boundaries and the public, immutable environmental telemetry arrays (The Aegis Protocol).
quotes.concern: Public entities must own critical data center infrastructure and environmental monitoring

quotes.text: Proprietary software-locking or subscription-based licensing of core utility interactions by private tech operators is strictly prohibited.
quotes.concern: Ban proprietary software control of utility-related data center systems

quotes.text: Simple Payback Period = Upfront Capital Premium divided by Annual OpEx Savings = $23,000,000 / $5,900,000 = 3.9 Years.
quotes.concern: Proposed technology achieves financial payback within 3.9 years through operational savings

bins.name: Corporate monopoly over power infrastructure
bins.description: The filing opposes concentration of power infrastructure control by technology companies, arguing this monopolization forces local communities to subsidize upgrades while bearing environmental and health costs.

bins.name: Data center water consumption and depletion
bins.description: The filing opposes evaporative cooling systems in data centers that strain local aquifer levels and require massive water consumption.

bins.name: Legacy diesel backup system emissions
bins.description: The filing opposes diesel-based backup power systems that increase localized emissions from data centers.

bins.name: Shifting infrastructure costs to ratepayers
bins.description: The filing argues that without regulation, data center operators shift infrastructure upgrade costs and long-term liabilities to local ratepayers and communities.

bins.name: Alternative cooling and power technologies for data centers
bins.description: The filing supports deployment of supercritical CO2 cooling loops, cryogenic purification, solid-state transformers, and active EMF cancellation to eliminate water consumption and emissions.

bins.name: Co-located large load facility standards
bins.description: The filing supports establishing environmental and infrastructure control standards for co-located data centers, including technology deployment, public ownership of infrastructure, and open-source utility interfaces.

bins.name: Open-source mandate for data center controls
bins.description: The filing supports requiring open-source utility interfaces for data centers over 20 MW, prohibiting proprietary software-locking of core systems, and publishing technical specifications under CC BY-SA 4.0.

bins.name: Public ownership of data center infrastructure
bins.description: The filing supports public utility and local regulatory authority ownership of solid-state transformers and environmental telemetry systems at data center facilities.

bins.name: Cost recovery and operational savings
bins.description: The filing claims the proposed technology achieves financial payback through annual operational savings of approximately 5.9 million dollars over a 3.9-year period.
